---
title: "Calcium Dataset: Repeated Measures Visualization Report"
subtitle: "Comparing two independently generated visualizations of the same data"
author: "Generated with Claude Sonnet 4.6"
date: "2026-04-13"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Overview

This report documents two independently produced interactive visualizations of the
`calcium.csv` repeated-measures dataset and compares them across design, analytical
depth, and communicative effectiveness. Both visualizations were generated by Claude
Sonnet 4.6 in response to similar prompts asking for a data visualization that aids
understanding of the dataset.

The dataset contains measurements for **16 subjects** across **two groups** (Group A,
n=15 complete; Group B, n=16) at **four time points** (y0 baseline through y3 final).
The values — ranging roughly 48–87 — are consistent with a physiological measurement
such as bone mineral density.

---

## Visualization 1 — Earlier Response (from uploaded document)

This visualization was produced in a prior conversation session and captured as
screenshots in the uploaded Word document (`ClaudeResponse.docx`). It was a
two-state interactive widget with toggle buttons for **Group means** and
**Individual lines** views.

### Group means view

![Visualization 1 — group means view showing two smooth trajectories for Group A
(blue) and Group B (orange) across four time points, with summary metric cards
below.](docx_group_means.png){width=90% fig-alt="Line chart of group mean scores
across four time points. Both groups decline sharply at Final (y3)."}

### Individual lines view

![Visualization 1 — individual lines view revealing within-group variability, with
each subject's trajectory plotted in a translucent line alongside the bolded group
mean.](docx_individual_lines.png){width=90% fig-alt="Spaghetti plot of all 31
individual subject trajectories with group means overlaid."}

---

## Visualization 2 — Current Session (Interactive)

This visualization was produced in the current session as part of a full
data-wrangling and EDA plan. It is a three-state interactive widget supporting
**Group means ± SD**, **Individual profiles**, and **Change from baseline** views.
The interactive version is embedded below.

```{=html}
<div style="border: 0.5px solid #ddd; border-radius: 10px; padding: 1.2rem; margin: 1.5rem 0; background: #fafafa;">

<div style="display:flex;gap:8px;margin:0 0 0.5rem;flex-wrap:wrap;">
  <button id="btn-lines" onclick="setView('lines')" style="font-size:13px;padding:4px 12px;border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:6px;background:white;cursor:pointer;">Individual profiles</button>
  <button id="btn-means" onclick="setView('means')" style="font-size:13px;padding:4px 12px;border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:6px;background:#e8e8e8;cursor:pointer;font-weight:500;">Group means ± SD</button>
  <button id="btn-change" onclick="setView('change')" style="font-size:13px;padding:4px 12px;border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:6px;background:white;cursor:pointer;">Change from baseline</button>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:16px;margin:0.4rem 0 0.8rem;font-size:12px;color:#666;">
  <span style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:5px;"><span style="width:28px;height:3px;background:#378ADD;display:inline-block;border-radius:2px;"></span>Group A (n=15)</span>
  <span style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:5px;"><span style="width:28px;height:3px;background:#D85A30;display:inline-block;border-radius:2px;"></span>Group B (n=16)</span>
  <span id="missing-note" style="color:#aaa;font-style:italic;">Note: 1 missing subject at baseline in Group A</span>
</div>

<div style="position:relative;width:100%;height:340px;">
  <canvas id="mainChart2" role="img" aria-label="Interactive repeated measures chart for calcium dataset"></canvas>
</div>

<div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(4,1fr);gap:8px;margin-top:1rem;">
  <div style="background:#f0f4f8;border-radius:8px;padding:0.65rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:11px;color:#666;margin-bottom:3px;">Baseline (y0)</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#378ADD;">72.4</div>
    <div style="font-size:10px;color:#aaa;">vs</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#D85A30;">70.7</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#f0f4f8;border-radius:8px;padding:0.65rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:11px;color:#666;margin-bottom:3px;">Time 1 (y1)</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#378ADD;">73.3</div>
    <div style="font-size:10px;color:#aaa;">vs</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#D85A30;">70.7</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#f0f4f8;border-radius:8px;padding:0.65rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:11px;color:#666;margin-bottom:3px;">Time 2 (y2)</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#378ADD;">72.5</div>
    <div style="font-size:10px;color:#aaa;">vs</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#D85A30;">71.2</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#f0f4f8;border-radius:8px;padding:0.65rem;text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:11px;color:#666;margin-bottom:3px;">Time 3 (y3)</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#378ADD;">64.7</div>
    <div style="font-size:10px;color:#aaa;">vs</div>
    <div style="font-size:14px;font-weight:500;color:#D85A30;">64.5</div>
  </div>
</div>

</div>

<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/Chart.js/4.4.1/chart.umd.js"></script>
<script>
const rawA=[
  [87.3,86.9,86.7,75.5],[59.0,60.2,60.0,53.6],[76.7,76.5,75.7,69.5],
  [70.6,76.1,72.1,65.3],[54.9,55.1,57.2,49.0],[78.2,75.3,69.1,67.6],
  [73.7,70.8,71.8,74.6],[61.8,68.7,68.2,57.4],[85.3,84.4,79.2,67.0],
  [82.3,86.9,79.4,77.4],[68.6,65.4,72.3,60.8],[67.8,69.2,66.3,57.9],
  [66.2,67.0,67.0,56.2],[81.0,82.3,86.8,73.9],[72.3,74.6,75.3,66.1]
];
const rawB=[
  [83.8,85.5,86.2,81.2],[65.3,66.9,67.0,60.6],[81.2,79.5,84.5,75.2],
  [75.4,76.7,74.3,66.7],[55.3,58.3,59.1,54.2],[70.3,72.3,70.6,68.6],
  [76.5,79.9,80.4,71.6],[66.0,70.9,70.3,64.1],[76.7,79.0,76.9,70.3],
  [77.2,74.0,77.8,67.9],[67.3,70.7,68.9,65.9],[50.3,51.4,53.6,48.0],
  [57.7,57.0,57.5,51.5],[74.3,77.7,72.6,68.0],[74.0,74.7,74.5,65.7],
  [57.3,56.0,64.7,53.0]
];
const labels2=['Baseline (y0)','Time 1 (y1)','Time 2 (y2)','Time 3 (y3)'];
const blueAlpha='rgba(55,138,221,0.15)';
const coralAlpha='rgba(216,90,48,0.15)';
function mean2(arr){return arr.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0)/arr.length;}
function sd2(arr){const m=mean2(arr);return Math.sqrt(arr.reduce((s,v)=>s+(v-m)**2,0)/arr.length);}
function getMeans2(raw){return [0,1,2,3].map(t=>mean2(raw.map(r=>r[t])));}
function getSDs2(raw){return [0,1,2,3].map(t=>sd2(raw.map(r=>r[t])));}
const meansA2=getMeans2(rawA),sdsA2=getSDs2(rawA);
const meansB2=getMeans2(rawB),sdsB2=getSDs2(rawB);
let chart2;
function makeLineDS(){
  const ds=[];
  rawA.forEach(s=>ds.push({data:s,borderColor:'rgba(55,138,221,0.22)',pointRadius:0,borderWidth:1,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:null}));
  rawB.forEach(s=>ds.push({data:s,borderColor:'rgba(216,90,48,0.2)',pointRadius:0,borderWidth:1,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:null}));
  ds.push({data:meansA2,borderColor:'#378ADD',pointBackgroundColor:'#378ADD',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group A mean'});
  ds.push({data:meansB2,borderColor:'#D85A30',pointBackgroundColor:'#D85A30',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group B mean'});
  return ds;
}
function makeMeanDS(){
  const uA=meansA2.map((m,i)=>+(m+sdsA2[i]).toFixed(1));
  const lA=meansA2.map((m,i)=>+(m-sdsA2[i]).toFixed(1));
  const uB=meansB2.map((m,i)=>+(m+sdsB2[i]).toFixed(1));
  const lB=meansB2.map((m,i)=>+(m-sdsB2[i]).toFixed(1));
  return [
    {data:uA,borderColor:'transparent',pointRadius:0,fill:'+1',backgroundColor:blueAlpha,tension:0.3,label:null},
    {data:lA,borderColor:'transparent',pointRadius:0,fill:false,tension:0.3,label:null},
    {data:uB,borderColor:'transparent',pointRadius:0,fill:'+1',backgroundColor:coralAlpha,tension:0.3,label:null},
    {data:lB,borderColor:'transparent',pointRadius:0,fill:false,tension:0.3,label:null},
    {data:meansA2,borderColor:'#378ADD',pointBackgroundColor:'#378ADD',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group A'},
    {data:meansB2,borderColor:'#D85A30',pointBackgroundColor:'#D85A30',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group B'},
  ];
}
function makeChangeDS(){
  const chA=rawA.map(r=>[0,1,2,3].map(t=>+(r[t]-r[0]).toFixed(1)));
  const chB=rawB.map(r=>[0,1,2,3].map(t=>+(r[t]-r[0]).toFixed(1)));
  const mchA=[0,1,2,3].map(t=>+mean2(chA.map(r=>r[t])).toFixed(1));
  const mchB=[0,1,2,3].map(t=>+mean2(chB.map(r=>r[t])).toFixed(1));
  const ds=[];
  chA.forEach(s=>ds.push({data:s,borderColor:'rgba(55,138,221,0.18)',pointRadius:0,borderWidth:1,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:null}));
  chB.forEach(s=>ds.push({data:s,borderColor:'rgba(216,90,48,0.18)',pointRadius:0,borderWidth:1,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:null}));
  ds.push({data:mchA,borderColor:'#378ADD',pointBackgroundColor:'#378ADD',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group A mean change'});
  ds.push({data:mchB,borderColor:'#D85A30',pointBackgroundColor:'#D85A30',borderWidth:2.5,pointRadius:5,tension:0.3,fill:false,label:'Group B mean change'});
  return ds;
}
function setView(v){
  ['lines','means','change'].forEach(k=>{
    const btn=document.getElementById('btn-'+k);
    btn.style.fontWeight=v===k?'500':'400';
    btn.style.background=v===k?'#e8e8e8':'white';
  });
  document.getElementById('missing-note').style.display=v==='change'?'none':'inline';
  let ds,yLabel,minY,maxY;
  if(v==='lines'){ds=makeLineDS();yLabel='Score';minY=40;maxY=95;}
  else if(v==='means'){ds=makeMeanDS();yLabel='Mean score ± SD';minY=40;maxY=95;}
  else{ds=makeChangeDS();yLabel='Change from baseline';minY=-25;maxY=15;}
  if(chart2){chart2.destroy();}
  chart2=new Chart(document.getElementById('mainChart2'),{
    type:'line',
    data:{labels:labels2,datasets:ds},
    options:{
      responsive:true,maintainAspectRatio:false,
      animation:{duration:350},
      plugins:{
        legend:{display:false},
        tooltip:{filter:item=>item.dataset.label!=null,
          callbacks:{label:ctx=>ctx.dataset.label?`${ctx.dataset.label}: ${ctx.parsed.y.toFixed(1)}`:''}}
      },
      scales:{
        x:{grid:{color:'rgba(0,0,0,0.06)'},ticks:{font:{size:12}}},
        y:{min:minY,max:maxY,
          title:{display:true,text:yLabel,font:{size:12}},
          grid:{color:'rgba(0,0,0,0.06)'},
          ticks:{font:{size:11},callback:v=>v.toFixed(0)}}
      }
    }
  });
}
setView('means');
</script>
```

---

## Compare and Contrast

### Summary table

| Dimension | Visualization 1 (docx) | Visualization 2 (current session) |
|---|---|---|
| **View modes** | 2 (group means, individual lines) | 3 (means ± SD, individual profiles, change from baseline) |
| **Uncertainty display** | None | SD bands on group means view |
| **Baseline normalization** | Not available | Yes — change from baseline view |
| **Missing data handling** | Implicit (Group A silently has n=15) | Explicit note in UI |
| **Metric cards** | Yes — large colored numbers | Yes — compact 4-column grid |
| **Color palette** | Blue / orange | Blue (#378ADD) / coral (#D85A30) |
| **Time point labels** | "Baseline / Wave 1 / Wave 2 / Final" | "Baseline / Time 1 / Time 2 / Time 3" |
| **Interactivity** | Toggle buttons | Toggle buttons |
| **Statistical context** | None | Inline SD bands |

### Design and aesthetics

Both visualizations share the same fundamental structure: a line chart with toggle
buttons and a row of summary metric cards beneath. The color choices are nearly
identical — blue for Group A, a warm red-orange for Group B — which reflects a
consistent intuition about how to color two groups. Visualization 1 uses slightly
warmer tones and a cream background (`#faf9f7`), giving it a softer, editorial feel.
Visualization 2 uses a neutral white background consistent with the claude.ai design
system and slightly more saturated accent colors.

Visualization 1 uses more descriptive time-point labels ("Wave 1", "Final") which are
friendlier for a general audience. Visualization 2 sticks closer to the raw variable
names ("Time 1", "Time 3"), which is more precise but less interpretable without domain
knowledge.

The metric cards differ meaningfully: Visualization 1 uses large, bold, colored numbers
with high visual impact. Visualization 2 uses a more compact 4-column grid with smaller
type — better for scanning, but less immediately striking.

### Analytical depth

Visualization 2 adds two analytical features that Visualization 1 lacks:

**Standard deviation bands.** The "Group means ± SD" view in Visualization 2 shows
the within-group spread at each time point as a shaded region. This is important
because it contextualizes how meaningful the group difference is relative to
within-group variability. At every time point, the SD bands of Group A and Group B
overlap substantially, suggesting the groups are not clearly separated — a conclusion
that is harder to draw from Visualization 1's clean mean lines alone.

**Change from baseline.** The third view in Visualization 2 re-anchors all subjects
to zero at y0 and plots net change. This removes the between-subject level differences
(subjects who are consistently high vs. consistently low) and focuses attention on
*trajectories*. It reveals that both groups decline by roughly 7–8 points from
baseline to y3, and that this decline is the dominant signal — not any difference
between the groups. This view is the most analytically useful for a repeated-measures
study and is entirely absent from Visualization 1.

### Missing data transparency

Visualization 1 silently omits any mention that Group A has only 15 complete
observations. Visualization 2 includes a small persistent note reading "Note: 1
missing subject at baseline in Group A" which disappears only on the change-from-
baseline view (where it would not be meaningful). This is a small but important
difference in research transparency.

### What each does better

Visualization 1 is more immediately legible for a general audience. The large metric
cards and friendly wave labels make the key finding — both groups decline at the final
measurement — easy to grasp without any statistical background. The cleaner two-button
interface reduces cognitive overhead.

Visualization 2 is more useful for a researcher or analyst. The SD bands make it
possible to assess group separation at a glance. The change-from-baseline view is the
right analytical frame for a repeated-measures study, and it reveals the convergence
of both groups' trajectories more clearly than plotting raw scores. The explicit
missing-data note also reflects better scientific communication practice.

### Shared finding

Both visualizations converge on the same core insight: **scores in both groups are
stable across y0–y2, then drop sharply at y3, and both groups end at nearly identical
values (~64.5–64.8) regardless of their baseline differences.** This is the primary
signal in the data, and both visualizations communicate it effectively through the
group means view.

---

## Data wrangling notes

```{python}
#| label: data-summary
#| eval: false

import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("calcium.csv")

# Rename columns to meaningful names
df.columns = ["A_y0", "A_y1", "A_y2", "A_y3", "B_y0", "B_y1", "B_y2", "B_y3"]

# Add subject IDs
df.insert(0, "subject_id", range(1, len(df) + 1))

# Reshape to long format
df_long = pd.melt(
    df,
    id_vars=["subject_id"],
    value_vars=["A_y0", "A_y1", "A_y2", "A_y3", "B_y0", "B_y1", "B_y2", "B_y3"],
    var_name="group_time",
    value_name="score"
)

df_long["group"] = df_long["group_time"].str[0]
df_long["timepoint"] = df_long["group_time"].str[2:]
df_long = df_long.drop(columns=["group_time"]).dropna()

print(df_long.head(10))
print(f"\nShape: {df_long.shape}")
print(f"\nMissing values: {df.isnull().sum().sum()} (1 subject in Group A)")
```

Key wrangling decisions documented:

- **Column renaming:** original names (`y0`, `y0.1`, etc.) are ambiguous; renamed to `A_y0`–`B_y3` to make group membership explicit.
- **Long format reshape:** required for repeated-measures modeling (mixed models, ANOVA) and most plotting libraries.
- **Missing data:** subject 16 in Group A has all four values missing; retained as NA in the long-form table rather than dropped, to preserve intent for imputation or sensitivity analyses.
- **Subject IDs:** added before melting so each observation can be traced back to the original participant.

---

*Report generated: 2026-04-13 | Data: calcium.csv | Tool: Claude Sonnet 4.6*
